data.title: US VP Vance says war between India and Pakistan will be 'none of our business'
data.description:
Key points:
  • JD Vance says US wants India and Pakistan to de-escalate
  • Vance says 'we can't control these countries'
  • US has held talks with both sides in recent days

U.S. Vice President JD Vance said on Thursday that India and Pakistan should de-escalate tensions, but he added that the U.S. cannot control the nuclear-armed Asian neighbors and a war between them would be "none of our business".

"We want this thing to de-escalate as quickly as possible. We can't control these countries, though," Vance said in an interview on Fox News show "The Story with Martha MacCallum."

"What we can do is try to encourage these folks to de-escalate a little bit, but we're not going to get involved in the middle of war that's fundamentally none of our business and has nothing to do with America's ability to control it," he added.

India is an important partner for Washington, which aims to counter China's rising influence, while Pakistan remains a U.S. ally despite its diminished importance after Washington's withdrawal from neighboring Afghanistan in 2021.

Analysts and some former officials have said U.S. involvement to achieve diplomatic goals in Russia's war in Ukraine and Israel's war in Gaza may make Washington leave India and Pakistan on their own in the early days of their tensions, without much direct pressure from the U.S. government.

Pakistan and India accused each other of launching drone attacks, and Islamabad's defence minister said further retaliation was "increasingly certain," on the second day of major clashes on Thursday. Two days of fighting killed nearly four dozen people.

The latest escalation in the decades-old India-Pakistan rivalry began on April 22 when Islamist militants killed 26 people in India-administered Kashmir in an attack that New Delhi blamed on Islamabad, which denied the accusations and called for a neutral probe.

"Our hope and our expectation is that this is not going to spiral into a broader regional war or, God forbid, a nuclear conflict," Vance said on Thursday.

Washington has held regular talks with both in recent days, including on Thursday when Secretary of State Marco Rubio held calls with Pakistan's prime minister and India's foreign minister while urging them to de-escalate and have direct dialogue.

U.S. President Donald Trump called rising tensions a shame. On Wednesday, he said he hoped the two countries will stop now after going "tit-for-tat." The State Department urged both countries to work towards what Washington terms as a "responsible solution."

data.title: Sula Vineyards to Invest Up to INR150 Million in Artisan Spirits via Rights Issue
data.description:
Sula Vineyards will invest up to INR150 million in its wholly owned subsidiary, Artisan Spirits, through a rights issue of 11.31 million shares at INR 13.26 each.

The funds aim to strengthen Artisan's net worth and be used to repay loans to the parent. The transaction, cleared by the audit committee, is expected to be completed by June 30.

Artisan reported revenue of INR849.2 million in FY25, up from INR668.6 million in the previous year.

data.title: Assured Guaranty Ltd reports results for the quarter ended March 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Assured Guaranty Ltd reported quarterly adjusted earnings of $3.18​​ per share for the quarter ended March 31, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of $1.96. The mean expectation of five analysts for the quarter was for earnings of $2.67 per share. Wall Street expected results to range from $1.66 to $3.15 per share.

  • Revenue rose 40.8% to $345.00 million from a year ago; analysts expected $241.10 million.

  • Assured Guaranty Ltd's reported EPS for the quarter was $3.44​.

  • Assured Guaranty Ltd shares had fallen by 0.5% this quarter and lost 2.6% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 75.9% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 4 "strong buy" or "buy," 1 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the property & casualty insurance peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Assured Guaranty Ltd is $105.00

data.title: OGN STOCK ALERT: Levi & Korsinsky Notifies Organon & Co. Investors of an Ongoing Investigation
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / May 8, 2025 / Levi & Korsinsky notifies investors that it has commenced an investigation of Organon & Co. ("Organon & Co.") concerning possible violations of federal securities laws.

On May 1, 2025, Organon reported mixed Q1 FY25 results, missing expectations on revenue decline of 7% y/y to $1.51B. The majority of the decline was attributed to a 17% y/y decline in Biosimilars and an 11% y/y drop in Established Brands on the loss of Atozet exclusivity. On the back of these results, OGN announced it was cutting its dividend from $0.28 to $0.02 (a decrease of more than 92%).

Following this news, the price of Organon stock fell more than 28% on the same day. To obtain additional information, go to:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/organon-co-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=147654&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. either via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or by telephone at (212)363-7500.

data.title: JGB Market Likely to Trade in Range for Now, Barclays Says — Market Talk
data.description: The JGB market is likely to trade in a range for now, say two members of Barclays FICC Research in a research report. The pricing for Bank of Japan rate increases is likely to take time to recover as this will probably require more certainty over U.S.-Japan trade talks and U.S. policy, the members say. Attractiveness of 10-year JGB yields perceived in terms of risk-adjusted carry roll-down should provide support to ranged trade, they say. While there could be potential upside in short-dated yields if the BOJ were to raise rates by January 2026, the short- and medium-term sector could remain rangebound for some time as this depends on outcome of trade policy, they add. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: South Korean Shares Open Flat
data.description:
The benchmark KOSPI traded flat around 2,579 points on Friday, but remained on track for a fourth consecutive weekly gain as investors assessed US trade talks with major economies.

President Trump announced a preliminary trade deal with the UK, with final details expected in the coming weeks.

He also hinted at a potential easing of tariffs on China, though uncertainty surrounding this weekend's trade talks in Switzerland weighed on optimism.

Domestically, traders responded positively to data showing a 23rd consecutive month of current account surplus in March, on the back of solid exports.

Among index heavyweights, automakers led the charge, including Hyundai Motor (2.2%) and Kia Corp (2.7%).

In corporate news, steelmaker Korea Zinc surged 8.4% after announcing plans to cancel over 2 million shares worth 1.82 trillion won to boost value.

On the downside, sharp losses were seen from SK Hynix (-1.2%), LG Energy Solutions (-2.6%), and KB Financial (-2.8%).

data.title: Malaysia Central Bank May Cut Rate in July Amid Global Risks — Market Talk
data.description: Malaysia's central bank appears on track to begin easing with a 25bp rate cut in July amid further signs of slowing growth, J.P. Morgan analyst Siddharth Jamad says in a note. While Bank Negara struck a balanced tone on growth and inflation remains manageable, he sees weakening momentum and trade tensions prompting a dovish policy shift. He expects a steady stream of 25bp cuts thereafter, with the policy rate likely reaching 2.25% by year-end and 2.00% in 1Q 2026, reflecting J.P. Morgan's baseline view of a U.S. and global recession in 2H this year. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: IRB Infrastructure Toll Revenue Up 10% to INR5.54 Billion in April 2025
data.description:
IRB Infrastructure Developers and its private InvIT arm reported toll revenue of 5.54 billion Indian rupees in April, a 10% increase from 5.03 billion rupees a year ago, according to a Thursday filing.

The Mumbai-based road developer noted strong momentum from FY25, which saw aggregate toll revenue rise to 63.60 billion Indian rupees, compared with 51.69 billion Indian rupees in FY24.

Notable performers included IRB MP Expressway, which generated 1.51 billion Indian rupees, and IRB Golconda Expressway with 683 million Indian rupees.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Thursday at 9 PM ET
data.description:
Stocks Gain After Trump Touts U.K. Deal, High Hopes on China

The Dow industrials climb 0.6%; Nasdaq adds 1.1%.

With U.K. Deal, U.S. Signals That 10% Tariff on World Is New Baseline

Both countries are hailing this as a good outcome, showing how much the landscape has shifted.

Trump's Tariffs Turned a Sleepy Industry Into the Front Lines of the Trade War

For years, customs brokers have helped companies calculate tariffs and expedite imports. The trade war has turned their business into a game of whack-a-mole.

U.S. and U.K. Unveil Framework for Trade Deal

President Trump lauded the agreement as the first with a major trading partner since he imposed blanket tariffs on countries worldwide.

New York Fed Survey Finds Consumers Ambivalent on Inflation Outlook

Consumers sent mixed signals about their inflation expectations in the New York Fed's April survey. But the poll showed no serious rise in long-term inflation expectations.

Canada's Financial Stability Faces Stiff Test From U.S. Tariffs, Bank of Canada Says

Annual central bank report says there is a risk permanent tariffs could cause high unemployment and business insolvencies.

Why the Fed Isn't Ready to Join Other Central Banks in Cutting Rates

The U.S. faces risks of higher inflation and unemployment. Countries without tax hikes on imports are focused on the hit to growth.

EU Targets American Aircraft, Car Parts for Possible Tariffs if Talks Fail

The bloc said it could target more than $100 billion worth of American products with tariffs if negotiations with the U.S. break down.

Bank of England Cuts Rates After Fed Stands Pat

The Bank of England cut rates, highlighting a growing gap in how global central banks and the Federal Reserve are approaching Trump's tariffs.

Jobless Claims Fell Last Week

The Labor Department said that 228,000 filed new jobless claims in the week through May 3, down from 241,000 a week earlier. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had expected to see 230,000 new filings.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Thursday at 9 PM ET
data.description:
Lyft Swings to Profit Amid Increase in Ridership

San Francisco-based company said rides grew 16% year over year, to 218.4 million, ahead of analyst views for 215.1 million.

McKesson to Spin Off Medical-Surgical Company

The pharmaceutical distributor said the new company would be a differentiated medical surgical supply and solutions business.

Paramount Notches Streaming Growth, but Revenue Drops

The company saw gains in its streaming business, but its overall revenue declined compared with the same period last year when it televised the Super Bowl.

News Corp Revenue Boosted by Gains at Dow Jones, HarperCollins

Digital and print Wall Street Journal subscriptions totaled 4.34 million for the March quarter, the company says.

CoStar Agrees to Buy Australian Advertiser Domain for $1.9 Billion

U.S. real-estate analytics provider CoStar agreed to buy property advertiser Domain in a sweetened deal.

Peloton Slashes Marketing Again in Hard Play for Profitability

Double-digit percentage cuts continue for the fourth consecutive quarter as the fitness company's marketing chief leaves.

Coinbase Reports Drop in Profit

Earlier in the day Coinbase announced a $2.9 billion cash-and-stock deal to acquire Deribit, a bitcoin options exchange.

TKO Group Raises Full-Year Outlook on Recent Acquisitions, Swings to Profit

The new forecasts came as TKO Group swung to a profit and posted higher-than-expected revenue in the recent quarter.

Air Canada Loss Widens, Full-Year Outlook Lowered on Economic Uncertainty

Chief Executive Michael Rousseau said he expects market conditions to be unsteady moving forward as the economic outlook appears uncertain

Funko Pulls Outlook as Loss Widens, Sales Fall

The company said it accelerated plans to diversify its supply chain, especially with regard to imports from China.

data.title: Nissan abandons plan for $1.1 billion EV battery plant in southern Japan
data.description:
Nissan Motor said on Friday it would abandon a plan to build a $1.1 billion factory for electric vehicle batteries on Japan's southwestern island of Kyushu, marking the latest change of plans for the troubled automaker.

Japan's third-biggest automaker had announced in January the plan for a lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery plant in the city of Kitakyushu that was set to create about 500 jobs with an investment of 153.3 billion yen ($1.05 billion).

"Nissan is taking immediate turnaround actions and exploring all options to recover its performance," the company said in a statement.

"After careful consideration of (the) investment efficiency, we have decided to cancel the construction of a new LFP battery plant in Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture."

The plant was supposed to start supply in July 2028 or later and have an annual production capacity of 5 gigawatt-hours (GWh), materials posted on Japan's industry ministry's website showed.

New CEO Ivan Espinosa is currently restructuring Nissan's operations. The company is shedding employees, reducing production capacity and closing plants.

Nissan said last month it expects a record net loss of 700 billion yen to 750 billion yen ($4.80 billion-$5.14 billion) for the financial year that ended in March due to impairment charges.

($1 = 145.8000 yen)

data.title: Trump Picks Fox News Host Jeanine Pirro as Washington, D.C.'s Top Federal Prosecutor — WSJ
data.description:
By Sadie Gurman

WASHINGTON — President Trump said he would name Fox News host Jeanine Pirro to be Washington's top federal prosecutor on an interim basis, after his first choice for the job failed to gather enough Republican support.

"Jeanine is incredibly well qualified for this position," Trump said on Truth Social Thursday, pointing to her television experience as well as her work as a judge and prosecutor in Westchester County, New York. "She is in a class by herself."

Hours earlier, Trump said he was abandoning his nomination of Ed Martin, to lead the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Columbia. Several Republicans said they couldn't support Martin because of his advocacy for members of the pro-Trump mob that stormed the U.S. Capitol.

Pirro, who joined Fox News in 2006, co-hosts the network's show "The Five" on weeknights. She is the latest in a series of Trump appointees who have held on-camera roles at Fox. The president had previously selected another Fox News host, Pete Hegseth, as secretary of defense, while former Fox Business host Sean Duffy serves as his transportation secretary.

Fox Corp. and Wall Street Journal parent News Corp share common ownership.

Pirro, 73 years old, has been close with Trump for decades and frequently defended him on her talk show. At least for now, she will oversee an office that has led some of the country's most politically charged prosecutions in recent years, including those of several Trump allies, which had put it squarely in the president's crosshairs. Trump didn't say whether he was considering nominating her for the role permanently.

She stepped down as district attorney in 2005 to unsuccessfully pursue higher office. Like Martin, she has supported Trump's push to seek retribution against perceived enemies and has backed his challenges to federal judges who have struck down immigration and other policies.

She was among the Fox hosts named in a lawsuit brought by Dominion Voting Systems after Trump and his allies alleged the company was engaged in a conscious effort to skew the 2020 race toward Joe Biden. The network settled the case and acknowledged false comments by Pirro and others.

Still, Pirro, who has experience as a prosecutor, is a more conventional choice than Martin, 54, who was a lightning rod from the outset. The Missouri lawyer and former chair of the state's Republican Party had been among the most vocal advocates for the Jan. 6 rioters, which drew concern from both Democratic and Republican lawmakers.

The implosion of Martin's nomination was a rare setback for Trump, who otherwise has been able to install some of his most polarizing picks for high-level posts. But Martin will maintain at least some influence. Trump said later Thursday that he had named Martin to be the director of a new working group on what the president and his allies call the weaponization of the Justice Department under former President Joe Biden, as well as associate deputy attorney general and pardon attorney, which vets and suggests candidates for clemency to the White House.

"In these highly important roles, Ed will make sure we finally investigate the weaponization of our government under the Biden regime and provide much needed justice for its victims," Trump said on Truth Social.

"Thank you, Mr. President," Martin wrote on X after the announcement. "We will MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!"

While leading the U.S. attorney's office in Washington, D.C., on an interim basis, Martin had begun to position himself as a leader in Trump's retribution campaign against perceived enemies and adversaries, promising investigations of people opposed to Trump and his administration.

Trump subsequently nominated Martin to serve in the role permanently, which requires Senate confirmation. It became clear Martin's nomination was in trouble earlier this week when Sen. Thom Tillis (R., N.C.) told the White House he would oppose his confirmation, essentially killing Martin's prospects weeks before his temporary appointment to the post expired. At least a half dozen other Republican senators had also privately expressed opposition, people familiar with the discussions said.

"He wasn't getting the support from people that I thought" he should have, Trump said Thursday. "I was disappointed, but that's the way it works sometimes," the president added. "He wasn't rejected, but we felt it would be hard."

During his short tenure in the U.S. attorney's office, Martin fired or demoted veteran attorneys who had prosecuted Jan. 6 defendants and began reviewing the department's decision to charge hundreds of them with felony offenses for obstructing an official congressional proceeding. Trump granted clemency to all of the more than 1,500 people charged in the attack. Martin, who had defended some of the rioters while in private practice, oversaw the dismissal of prosecutions that were pending.

His nomination hit another hurdle after it emerged that he had appeared more than 150 times on the Russian propaganda networks RT and Sputnik, including a 2021 segment discussing whether the U.S. was sliding into authoritarianism. Lawmakers also cited concerns over his ties to Timothy Hale-Cusanelli, a Jan. 6 rioter known to espouse white supremacist and antisemitic views. Martin had since tried to distance himself.

Write to Sadie Gurman at sadie.gurman@wsj.com
